finance-markets.title: US Futures, Korean Stocks Drop as AI Mania Fades: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Stock markets across Asia and the US are experiencing a significant slowdown after months of explosive growth driven by artificial intelligence (AI) excitement. The AI boom—where investors rushed to buy tech stocks believing AI would revolutionize industries—is cooling down as reality checks in. This matters because when markets shift this dramatically, it affects retirement savings, college funds, and even job prospects as companies reassess their spending. A pullback like this often signals that investors are becoming more cautious and questioning whether companies' AI promises are actually realistic or just hype.

finance-markets.title: Korean Stocks Slide as Cracks Emerge in World-Beating Rally
finance-markets.summary: South Korea's massive $4.9 trillion stock market—one of the world's largest—is showing warning signs after an incredible winning streak. The country's stock exchange (called KOSPI) had been outperforming every other major market globally, but now investors are noticing problems underneath the surface, like uneven participation where only certain sectors are rising. This is crucial because South Korea is a tech powerhouse (Samsung, LG, etc.), and when its markets stumble, it signals global tech problems ahead. When major markets start cracking, it can spread like dominoes to other countries' stock markets and affect international investment flows.

finance-markets.title: Goldman Sachs expects SpaceX's AI revenue to increase 100-fold by 2030
finance-markets.summary: Goldman Sachs, one of the world's most influential investment banks, is projecting that Elon Musk's SpaceX will dramatically increase its artificial intelligence revenue from current levels to 100 times larger by 2030. This prediction is backing up SpaceX's asking price of $1.78 trillion for its planned initial public offering (IPO)—basically when a private company sells shares to the public on the stock market for the first time. The huge valuation matters because it shows how much investors believe AI will transform space technology and internet services. However, such extreme projections can be risky; if SpaceX doesn't deliver, investors who buy shares could lose enormous amounts of money.

finance-markets.title: Bitcoin tumbles after Strategy sale unnerves crypto traders
finance-markets.summary: Bitcoin, the world's most famous cryptocurrency (digital money not controlled by banks), has dropped sharply after a major holder sold a large portion of their holdings. A cryptocurrency is basically digital currency that uses computer codes to verify transactions instead of banks. This sale spooked traders—people who buy and sell to make quick profits—and is contributing to Bitcoin's worst week since November 2022, showing how fragile crypto investor confidence can be. For teenagers interested in crypto, this demonstrates that even though Bitcoin seems revolutionary, its price can swing wildly based on what a few large holders decide to do, making it extremely risky compared to traditional stocks.

finance-markets.title: Fuel Shocks, Rising Prices Threaten India's Consumption Growth
finance-markets.summary: India, home to 1.4 billion people and the world's fifth-largest economy, is facing a serious problem: surging global energy prices are making fuel and everyday items more expensive, which threatens to slow down spending by India's rapidly growing middle class. When fuel prices spike globally (often due to conflicts or supply disruptions), countries that rely on importing oil—like India—get hit hard because everything from transportation to electricity becomes costlier. This matters because India's economic growth depends heavily on its middle-class consumers buying goods and services; if they stop spending due to higher prices, it weakens the entire economy. Additionally, economic slowdowns in major countries like India can ripple globally and affect prices and job opportunities worldwide.

finance-markets.title: Bond Traders Expecting Fed Hike Look Ahead to Jobs Data Risk
finance-markets.summary: Bond traders (people who buy and sell government bonds, which are loans to governments that pay interest) are betting that the Federal Reserve—America's central bank that controls interest rates—will raise rates over the next year. Bonds and interest rates have an inverse relationship: when rates rise, bond prices fall, potentially costing traders billions. The traders are nervously watching upcoming US employment data because if job numbers are weak, the Fed might not raise rates after all, which would tank their investments. This matters to teenagers because Federal Reserve interest rate decisions affect everything from student loan rates to mortgage costs to job availability; understanding this connection helps explain why financial markets react so intensely to employment reports.

finance-markets.title: Trump's pledge to unleash a 'golden age' of US manufacturing sputters
finance-markets.summary: Former President Donald Trump promised to bring manufacturing jobs back to America and spark a 'golden age' of US factory production, but the reality is falling short. Corporate spending on new factories has actually declined, and industry executives report an uneven recovery—meaning some sectors are growing while others are struggling. This matters because manufacturing jobs traditionally provide stable middle-class income without requiring college degrees, so when manufacturing sputters, it affects economic opportunity for millions. Trump's unfulfilled promise also shows how difficult it is to reverse decades of companies moving production overseas due to lower labor costs, demonstrating that political promises about the economy must be viewed skeptically.

finance-markets.title: SpaceX IPO shows Musk's genius is in mythmaking
finance-markets.summary: While SpaceX is planning to go public and raise billions of dollars by selling shares, critics argue that Elon Musk is essentially selling an elaborate story rather than proven financial success. 'Mythmaking' means creating a compelling narrative that captures people's imaginations—Musk is brilliant at this—but the actual business plan to support SpaceX's $1.78 trillion asking price isn't entirely clear. This matters because it highlights a dangerous trend in modern finance: investors sometimes buy into visionary leaders' stories without fully understanding the companies' fundamentals (basic financial health and realistic projections). For teenagers learning about investing, this is a critical lesson: charismatic CEOs and exciting missions don't guarantee profitable companies, and even famous entrepreneurs can ask for valuations that don't add up mathematically.

finance-markets.title: Tech groups find a new way to monetise AI hype: selling volatility
finance-markets.summary: Tech companies are issuing convertible bonds—special debt instruments that can be converted into company stock—at record levels, essentially profiting from the extreme price swings (volatility) happening in markets right now. Volatility means large, unpredictable price fluctuations; when markets are twitchy and jumping around dramatically, convertible bonds become more valuable because they offer protection against stock price drops. Companies are essentially cashing in on investor anxiety and uncertainty by selling these complex financial products. This demonstrates how Wall Street creates ways to profit from market chaos, and it shows teenagers that during periods of extreme hype (like the current AI craze), sophisticated investors develop increasingly complex strategies to extract value, often at the expense of regular retail investors.

finance-markets.title: Europe is finally flexing its innovation muscles
finance-markets.summary: The European Union has launched a new tech sovereignty package showing a strategic shift from just defending against American tech dominance toward actively building Europe's own technology innovation capabilities. 'Tech sovereignty' means developing homegrown technology companies and not depending on US or Chinese tech giants for critical infrastructure and services. Previously, Europe focused mainly on regulatory defense (like strict privacy rules), but now it's taking offensive steps to create European AI companies, semiconductor manufacturers, and digital infrastructure. This matters globally because if Europe successfully builds independent tech capabilities, it reduces American and Chinese dominance and creates more competition, innovation, and choice for consumers worldwide. For teenagers, this signals a major geopolitical shift where technology leadership is becoming as important as military or economic power.

world-news.title: Zelensky proposes face-to-face talks in open letter to Putin
world-news.summary: Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelensky has publicly called for direct, one-on-one negotiations with Russian President Vladimir Putin to end the war that has devastated Ukraine since 2022. Zelensky, who has led Ukraine through nearly three years of Russian invasion, believes only personal dialogue between the two leaders can achieve a lasting peace. This proposal comes as the US shifts focus toward Iran amid broader Middle East tensions. The move signals Ukraine's willingness to negotiate while maintaining its sovereignty. Direct talks between warring leaders are rare but historically significant in ending conflicts. This matters because millions of Ukrainians have been displaced, and global food and energy prices remain affected by the ongoing war.

world-news.title: Hezbollah rejects renewed ceasefire agreed by Israel and Lebanon
world-news.summary: The United States brokered a ceasefire agreement between Israel and Lebanon, but Hezbollah—a powerful militant group and political party based in Lebanon—has rejected it. A ceasefire is a temporary halt to fighting agreed upon by opposing sides. Hezbollah, which has been in conflict with Israel for decades, said the terms don't meet its demands. This rejection means fighting will likely continue in the region. The group's refusal complicates US diplomatic efforts to reduce Middle East tensions. For teenagers, this matters because regional conflicts can affect global oil prices, refugee crises, and international security. When powerful armed groups reject peace deals, it signals that wars can persist despite international attempts to end them.

world-news.title: Iran war live: Hezbollah rejects truce as Israel continues Lebanon strikes
world-news.summary: As Hezbollah rejects a US-brokered ceasefire, Israel continues military strikes against the group in Lebanon, escalating Middle East tensions further. Israel's Supreme Court has also annulled a government ban preventing the International Red Cross from visiting Palestinian prisoners held in Israeli jails—a significant human rights development. The Red Cross is an independent organization that monitors treatment of prisoners and detainees in conflicts worldwide. This court decision means international observers can now document prisoner conditions. The ongoing strikes, combined with increased international oversight, reflect deepening regional instability. This matters globally because Middle East conflicts often spread regionally, affect oil markets, and create humanitarian crises. Israel, Lebanon, Palestine, and Iran are all interconnected in this escalating situation.

world-news.title: Trump hits back at 'unpatriotic' vote after House rebukes him over Iran
world-news.summary: The US House of Representatives—the lower chamber of Congress—passed a measure seeking to halt further military action against Iran, which President Trump criticized as 'unpatriotic.' Congress, which controls military spending and war declarations, used this vote to challenge Trump's authority over foreign military decisions. Though largely symbolic (meaning it's more of a statement than enforceable law), the vote reflects deep disagreement between Congress and the Trump administration over Iran policy. Trump, known for confrontational rhetoric, fired back at lawmakers. This matters because it shows checks and balances in the US government: Congress can constrain a president's war powers. For teenagers, understanding that Congress, not just the president, decides military action is crucial to how democracy prevents any one leader from having total power.

world-news.title: Democrat fails to block US measure to deepen Israel military cooperation
world-news.summary: Congressman Ro Khanna, a Democrat, attempted to block a measure that would strengthen military integration between the United States and Israel, but his effort failed. Khanna argued that deepening military cooperation primarily benefits Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's political goals rather than serving broader US interests. Military integration means coordinating weapons systems, intelligence sharing, and joint operations between two nations' armed forces. Despite Khanna's opposition, the measure advanced, showing that most Congress members support closer Israel-US ties. This vote reflects ongoing debate within the US government about the extent of support for Israel's military operations. It matters because US military aid influences Middle East power dynamics, affects Palestinian territories, and shapes American foreign policy priorities in a volatile region.

world-news.title: Why Mogadishu clashes are deepening Somalia's political crisis again
world-news.summary: Somalia's capital city, Mogadishu, is experiencing violent clashes that are worsening an already serious political crisis centered on disputed elections. Tensions erupted into street violence, leaving residents terrified and disrupting daily life in the city. Elections are the process by which citizens choose their leaders; when elections are disputed or unfair, it can delegitimize governments and spark conflict. Somalia has experienced decades of instability, and this new violence threatens to undo recent progress toward stability. The political crisis means different factions within Somalia's government are fighting for power rather than cooperating. This matters globally because state collapse in Somalia can enable piracy, terrorism, and refugee crises that affect the entire Horn of Africa region and beyond. International organizations monitor Somalia closely for these reasons.

world-news.title: Kenyan president defends US Ebola facility amid deadly protests
world-news.summary: Kenyan President William Ruto is defending his government's decision to allow the United States to build an Ebola quarantine facility on Kenyan soil, despite deadly protests against the project. An Ebola facility would serve as a containment center if the deadly virus spreads in East Africa. Ebola is a rare but extremely severe disease with high mortality rates that spreads through contact with infected blood or bodily fluids. Protesters oppose the facility, fearing it represents unwanted foreign control or could introduce the virus to Kenya. Ruto insists the facility is necessary for regional health security. This matters because it highlights tensions between national security needs and public trust in government decisions. Global health infrastructure requires cooperation between nations, but local populations must understand and support these measures for them to work effectively.

world-news.title: Why is the US so invested in Colombia's election?
world-news.summary: US officials are closely monitoring and reportedly involved in Colombia's presidential elections, raising questions about American influence in Latin American politics. Colombia is a strategically important South American nation that has historically been a US ally in the region. The US cares about Colombia's elections because they determine policies affecting drug trafficking, trade relationships, and regional stability. When powerful nations like the US publicly invest in other countries' elections, it raises concerns about sovereignty (a nation's right to self-govern without outside interference). US involvement typically favors pro-American candidates or policies. This matters because it reflects broader patterns of how powerful nations try to shape global politics to their advantage. Teenagers should understand that international relations often involve countries interfering in each other's affairs, which can undermine democracy and create resentment.

world-news.title: US defence secretary compares Bolivia protests to government 'overthrow'
world-news.summary: The US Defence Secretary has compared recent protests in Bolivia to a government overthrow attempt, signaling strong Trump administration support for Bolivian President Rodrigo Paz following his electoral victory. An electoral victory occurs when a candidate wins a presidential election. Paz defeated the left-wing MAS party (a socialist-oriented political movement) in the election. The Defence Secretary's inflammatory language suggests the US views Paz's opponents as illegitimate threats rather than legitimate political opposition. Bolivia has a history of military coups and political instability, making such rhetoric particularly significant. This matters because it shows how the US shapes Latin American politics by backing certain leaders militarily and diplomatically. When the US defence establishment takes sides in foreign elections this openly, it can destabilize regions and reduce democratic legitimacy of governments.

world-news.title: Hezbollah rejects US-brokered ceasefire deal between Israel and Lebanon
world-news.summary: Hezbollah, a militant organization and political party based in Lebanon, has formally rejected a ceasefire framework that both Israel and Lebanon accepted with US mediation. A ceasefire framework is a set of agreed-upon terms for halting military operations between conflicting parties. Hezbollah's rejection means one side refuses to stop fighting even though the other two parties (Israel and Lebanon's government) agreed to terms. This creates a complicated situation where Lebanon's government accepted peace but Hezbollah, which operates within Lebanon, continues warfare independently. Mediation is when a neutral third party (here, the US) helps negotiate between enemies. Hezbollah's defiance shows that ceasefire agreements only work when all armed parties accept them. This matters globally because when ceasefire attempts fail, conflicts persist, humanitarian crises worsen, and regional instability spreads across borders, affecting international security and economics.

tech-ai.title: TSMC struggles to keep up with AI demand: 'We can only support so much'
tech-ai.summary: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is the world's largest chipmaker—think of them as the factory that produces the brains of computers and AI systems. Right now, they're drowning in orders because everyone wants AI chips, but they physically can't make them fast enough. CEO C.C. Wei admitted that even with new factories being built in the United States, demand is so massive that TSMC might become a bottleneck (a chokepoint that slows everything down). This matters because without enough chips, tech companies can't build AI systems, which means delays for the products and services you use. It's like a restaurant that's so popular they can't cook food fast enough—customers have to wait longer for their meals.

tech-ai.title: Ahead of its IPO, Anthropic's Daniela Amodei shrugs off doubts about AI's returns
tech-ai.summary: Anthropic is an AI company founded by Daniela Amodei and others who previously worked at OpenAI (the company behind ChatGPT). An IPO (Initial Public Offering) means a company is about to sell shares to the public on the stock market, allowing regular people to own a piece of it. Anthropic grew explosively—their revenue jumped from $9 billion at the end of 2025 to an estimated $47 billion annualized by May 2026, which is absolutely stunning growth. However, people are questioning whether AI companies can actually make money long-term, because AI is expensive to build and maintain. Amodei is confident they'll prove doubters wrong. This matters because Anthropic is one of the biggest AI companies in the world, and their success or failure will shape how AI develops and who controls it.

tech-ai.title: Helion, the Sam Altman-backed fusion startup, raises $465M to build a power plant for Microsoft
tech-ai.summary: Helion is a startup trying to create fusion energy—basically mimicking the process that powers the sun to generate unlimited clean electricity. Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI (the company behind ChatGPT), is a major investor backing this company. Helion just raised $465 million in new funding to build an actual power plant that will supply electricity to Microsoft by 2028. This is huge because AI systems require enormous amounts of electricity, and traditional power plants pollute the environment. If fusion works, it could solve both problems at once. The fact that one of tech's most powerful people is betting billions on this shows how serious the industry is about finding new energy sources. This matters because your future—whether you get clean electricity or face energy shortages—depends on solving this problem.

tech-ai.title: Meta's Oversight Board says account bans lack due process, transparency
tech-ai.summary: Meta (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp's parent company) has an independent Oversight Board—basically an external review team that checks whether Meta is treating users fairly. The board recently criticized Meta for banning people's accounts without clear explanations or fair procedures. Due process means you deserve to know what you did wrong and have a chance to defend yourself before punishment. The board is also concerned that Meta uses AI systems to help decide whether to ban accounts, but won't explain how those AI systems work. This matters to you because it affects whether you could suddenly lose your Instagram or Facebook account without understanding why, and whether AI systems are secretly making decisions about your online life. It raises important questions about fairness and transparency in how tech companies control online spaces.

tech-ai.title: Meta steals a tactic from Tesla and builds data centers in tents
tech-ai.summary: Meta (Facebook's parent company) is trying to slash its massive electricity bills by building data centers—the massive buildings full of servers that store all your photos and run Instagram. They're literally using tents instead of expensive permanent buildings. Tesla, the electric car company, already figured out that tents are a clever temporary solution for manufacturing. Meta is copying this tactic because they need more computing power incredibly fast to handle AI systems, but don't have time or money to build traditional data centers. A data center is basically a giant warehouse full of computers that process information. While tents might sound cheap and temporary, they show how desperate tech companies are becoming to handle AI's massive power demands. This matters because it reveals the real costs hidden behind your free social media apps—and how companies are scrambling to afford the infrastructure.

tech-ai.title: Apple approves Poke as the first AI agent on its Messages for Business platform
tech-ai.summary: Poke is a startup that lets people use AI agents through text messages—basically, you can text an AI to get things done without downloading a separate app. An AI agent is software that can perform tasks for you independently, like booking appointments or answering questions. Apple just approved Poke as the first AI agent allowed on Messages for Business, which is Apple's platform for companies to communicate with customers. This is significant because Apple is traditionally very protective about what software runs on its devices, so approving an AI agent is a big deal. It signals that Apple is embracing AI in everyday communication tools. This matters because it shows how AI is moving from fancy research projects into the apps you actually use daily—soon you might chat with AI assistants as naturally as texting friends, integrated right into the apps on your phone.

tech-ai.title: Airbnb's Brian Chesky plans to launch a new AI lab
tech-ai.summary: Brian Chesky is the CEO of Airbnb, the massive company that lets people rent out their homes to travelers. He's announcing plans to create an AI lab—basically a research team dedicated to building and experimenting with artificial intelligence. Previously, Airbnb didn't make deals with existing AI companies (called LLM partnerships, where LLM stands for Large Language Model—the technology behind ChatGPT) because Chesky thought the AI products weren't good enough yet. Now he believes the technology has improved enough to build his own AI tools. This matters because it shows how every major tech company is racing to develop AI capabilities. Airbnb's AI could eventually help you find better vacation spots, automate customer service, or personalize your travel recommendations. It's part of a massive industry shift where every company wants to use AI to improve their business.

tech-ai.title: Filtr is a new privacy tool that blocks ads in almost every iPhone and Mac app
tech-ai.summary: Filtr is a new app for iPhones, iPads, and Mac computers that blocks advertisements from appearing inside other apps you use. Normally, apps show you ads and track your behavior to sell that information to advertisers—that's how many free apps make money. Filtr uses a feature in the latest Apple software to prevent ads from loading in the first place, which protects your privacy and makes apps run faster. This matters because most apps track everything you do—which videos you watch, which products you search for, where you go—and sell that data to advertisers. If you use Filtr, companies can't monitor your behavior as easily. It's a tool that gives you more control over your digital life and privacy. However, it also means the companies behind free apps lose money, so this could shake up how apps are funded in the future.

tech-ai.title: Windows is back on the Microsoft menu
tech-ai.summary: Satya Nadella is the CEO of Microsoft, the company that makes Windows (the operating system on most computers). At Microsoft's Build conference this week, Nadella made Windows a major focus for the first time in years, introducing new Surface hardware (the computer brand Microsoft makes) called the Surface RTX Spark Dev Kit. RTX refers to Nvidia chips that are specially designed for AI and graphics work. This announcement is important because for years, Microsoft seemed to care more about cloud computing than Windows itself. Now they're pivoting back because AI is becoming central to computing, and they want Windows to be the go-to platform for AI development. This matters because Windows runs on billions of computers globally, and if Microsoft successfully makes Windows the best platform for AI development, it affects what tools programmers use and ultimately what technology gets built.

tech-ai.title: Google is letting social media stars customize their search result page
tech-ai.summary: Google just launched a new feature that lets famous content creators customize how they appear when people search for them on Google Search. To qualify, you need at least 100,000 YouTube subscribers, 100,000 Instagram followers, 100,000 Twitter/X followers, or 300,000 TikTok followers, and be at least 18 years old. The feature lets you highlight your videos, articles, and links to other profiles. This matters because it shows Google recognizing that social media influencers and creators are powerful forces online, and they want to help them control their personal brand on Google Search. However, it also creates a gap between famous creators and regular people—most people won't qualify for this feature, so Google is giving special treatment only to those with massive followings. This raises questions about fairness and whether Google is becoming a platform that favors the already-famous even more.

science-space.title: Scientists finally crack an 'undruggable' pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
science-space.summary: For decades, pancreatic cancer has been a death sentence—most patients didn't survive long after diagnosis because doctors couldn't target the mutations driving the disease. Scientists at major research institutions have now developed a drug called daraxonrasib that attacks the KRAS mutation, which powers roughly 90% of pancreatic tumors. Researchers previously thought this mutation was 'undruggable' (impossible to treat with medicine), but this breakthrough proves them wrong. In clinical trials, the drug nearly doubled survival time for patients with advanced pancreatic cancer and reduced their death risk by 60%. This is a massive deal because pancreatic cancer is one of the deadliest cancers worldwide—this drug could save thousands of lives.

science-space.title: NASA-Funded Study Shows Wildfire Smoke's Hidden Ozone Toll
science-space.summary: Wildfires aren't just destroying forests—they're creating a hidden air quality problem that affects millions of people far from the flames. A NASA-funded research team discovered that wildfire smoke worsens ground-level ozone pollution (a toxic gas that forms when sunlight hits smoke chemicals) across much of the United States over the last decade. Ground-level ozone is different from the protective ozone layer in the upper atmosphere; this kind damages your lungs and causes respiratory problems, especially for kids and athletes. The study reveals that even if you live hundreds of miles away from active wildfires, the smoke can still poison your air quality weeks later. This matters because climate change is making wildfires bigger and more frequent, meaning more people will face dangerous air quality in the future.

science-space.title: Meteorite found in Sahara desert may be 1st evidence of lost solar system world
science-space.summary: Scientists discovered something mind-blowing in the Sahara Desert: a meteorite (a rock that fell from space) containing evidence of a moon-sized world that no longer exists. This lost planet formed just a few million years after our solar system was born, roughly 4.5 billion years ago, but was destroyed long ago—possibly by colliding with another planetary body. Researchers used advanced chemical analysis to determine the meteorite's origin and composition, which revealed it came from this mysterious vanished world. Finding physical evidence of lost planets is extremely rare because they were destroyed so long ago that usually nothing remains. This discovery helps scientists understand how planets form, collide, and evolve in young solar systems—knowledge that could help us find and study planets around distant stars.

science-space.title: Most exoplanets might be 'soot factories,' scientists say: 'Like you have a natural diesel engine'
science-space.summary: Imagine planets that are basically giant dirty engines—that's what scientists just discovered many distant worlds might be. A chemical engineer noticed that the hazy atmospheres (the air layers surrounding some planets orbiting other stars, called exoplanets) produce spectra (light signatures scientists use to identify chemicals) that look identical to soot created by diesel engines and combustion. These mini-Neptune planets (smaller than Jupiter but bigger than Earth) have thick, cloudy atmospheres filled with dark particles. The soot forms when chemical reactions break down methane and other gases high in the planets' atmospheres. This matters because it changes how scientists understand these distant worlds and what their weather and chemistry are actually like. It also gives us clues about how planets develop and what kinds of conditions might exist on worlds completely different from Earth.

science-space.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica's ice
science-space.summary: Beneath Antarctica's massive ice sheet, researchers discovered a giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins—essentially a colossal underground geological structure that had never been fully mapped before. Using advanced radar and satellite technology that can 'see' through ice, scientists realized that several well-known subglacial features (geographical formations hidden under ice) are actually all connected as one massive system. This discovery rewrites our understanding of Antarctica's ancient geological history, showing how the continent formed and shifted over millions of years. Why does this matter to you? Antarctica's ice sheet contains roughly 90% of Earth's fresh water, and understanding its structure helps scientists predict how it might melt or collapse as the planet warms due to climate change. Better predictions mean better planning for rising sea levels, which could affect coastal cities worldwide where billions of people live.

science-space.title: Glittering star cluster image reveals missing patch of stars: 'We were not looking for the gap, but we found it'
science-space.summary: The Euclid space telescope (a powerful observatory launched by the European Space Agency to map the universe) captured stunning images of NGC 6397, an ancient globular cluster—a spherical group of hundreds of thousands of old stars orbiting together in space. Scientists noticed something strange: a subtle gap or missing patch in the brightness pattern of red dwarf stars (small, dim stars that are incredibly common in the universe). Researchers weren't specifically looking for this gap, but the detailed imaging revealed it clearly. This missing patch could reveal important information about how stars migrate within clusters, how massive objects like black holes influence star movements, or how gravitational forces reshape these stellar neighborhoods. Understanding globular clusters helps astronomers piece together the universe's early history and how gravity shapes cosmic structures.

science-space.title: Goethe never knew this 40-million-year-old ant was hidden in his collection
science-space.summary: Scientists made a jaw-dropping discovery in amber (fossilized tree resin that preserves insects perfectly) from the personal collection of Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, the famous German writer and scientist who died in 1832. Researchers found three hidden fossil insects preserved in the amber, including an extinct ant species from 40 million years ago, captured in extraordinary detail. Using advanced 3D imaging technology, scientists could see not just the ant's outer body but also internal structures—muscle attachments, internal organs, and other details normally invisible in fossils. The discovery revealed this ant species likely built massive nests high in trees, providing new clues about its behavior and biology. This matters because ancient insects in amber provide windows into ecosystems from millions of years ago, helping us understand how life has evolved and how environments have changed over deep time.

science-space.title: Interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS is not an alien spacecraft: SETI hunt for 'technosignatures' comes up empty
science-space.summary: When the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS (a frozen space rock that originated from outside our solar system) was discovered, some people got excited wondering if it could be an alien spacecraft. SETI scientists (the Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence organization that hunts for signs of alien life) conducted a careful search for 'technosignatures'—radio signals or other signs of advanced alien technology. After searching thoroughly, they found absolutely nothing—no radio signals, no artificial patterns, no evidence of alien engineering. This finding strongly suggests 3I/ATLAS is a natural comet, just like the icy objects that occasionally visit our solar system from the distant universe. While no aliens were found this time, the SETI search matters because it represents humanity's ongoing effort to answer one of the biggest questions: Are we alone in the universe? Each search brings us closer to understanding whether life exists elsewhere.

science-space.title: August 2026 lunar eclipse: Everything you need to know about the 96% 'blood moon'
science-space.summary: Mark your calendar for August 27-28, 2026, because a spectacular deep partial lunar eclipse will be visible across North America. During this eclipse, roughly 96% of the Moon will slide into Earth's shadow, meaning it will turn a reddish color—that's why it's called a 'blood moon.' (The red color happens because Earth's atmosphere bends sunlight onto the Moon's surface, and our atmosphere filters out blue light, leaving only red.) This eclipse will be the best lunar eclipse visible from North America for years, and it won't be matched by another major eclipse until New Year's Eve 2028. A lunar eclipse is safe to watch with your naked eyes unlike a solar eclipse, and it's a stunning reminder of how our planet, Moon, and Sun move through space together. It's a great opportunity to photograph the Moon or simply experience the wonder of celestial mechanics.

science-space.title: NASA Hosts 2026 Review on Advanced Composite Manufacturing
science-space.summary: NASA's HiCAM project (which stands for Hi-Rate Composite Aircraft Manufacturing) brought together 150 engineers, scientists, and industry experts from 22 different organizations for a major review meeting at NASA's Langley Research Center in Virginia in May 2026. Composites are advanced materials made by combining two or more materials (like carbon fiber and resin) to create something stronger and lighter than the original materials alone—perfect for building spacecraft and aircraft. This public-private partnership means NASA works directly with companies to develop better manufacturing techniques that could revolutionize how we build spacecraft, rockets, and future aircraft. The review examined recent progress toward making composite manufacturing faster and more efficient. Why does this matter? Lighter aircraft and spacecraft use less fuel, cost less money to launch, and can carry more cargo or people. These advances could make space travel more affordable and accessible in the coming decades.

The “data-centre boom” is driving a surge in gas investment in the US, pushing its...

The world’s most comprehensive disaster database – relied on by thousands of climate scientists and...

energy-climate.title: Cybersecurity concerns put focus on India’s solar inverter supplies
energy-climate.summary:
While growing cybersecurity concerns have prompted the EU to consider restrictions on funding for PV projects using inverters supplied by high-risk vendors like China, experts say India may need a more calibrated approach that strengthens security oversight without disrupting solar deployment.
The post Cybersecurity concerns put focus on India’s solar inverter supplies appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Malaysia forecast to reach 29.7 GW of solar by 2035

GlobalData’s latest analysis says Malaysia is on course to surpass its 2040 renewable energy targets by 2031.

energy-climate.title: US adds 6.4 GW of utility-scale solar, wind, storage in Q1
energy-climate.summary:
Report from the American Clean Power Association finds told clean power capacity in the U.S. hit 370 GW in Q1, enough to power 80 million homes, while the pipeline for new projects grew 6% over the previous year.
The post US adds 6.4 GW of utility-scale solar, wind, storage in Q1 appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Solar streetlights with AI could solve data center energy demand
energy-climate.summary:
Putting Nvidia computer chips into solar-powered streetlamps to create a virtual power plant is a simple solution to the energy demand problems created by data centers, according to Edward Fitzpatrick. His company ConFlow Power Group is doing exactly that with its iLamp streetlamps, he told pv magazine.
The post Solar streetlights with AI could solve data center energy demand appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: European Commission to conduct risk assessment of EU solar, wind installations
energy-climate.summary:
The European Commission says its new strategic roadmap for digitalization and AI in energy will address cybersecurity concerns by undertaking a risk assessment of solar and wind installations in the EU and reviewing the energy security of supply framework.
The post European Commission to conduct risk assessment of EU solar, wind installations appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: India extends countervailing duty on Malaysian solar glass imports for five years
energy-climate.summary:
The move follows a recommendation from India's Directorate General of Trade Remedies, which found that removing the duty would likely lead to the continuation or recurrence of subsidized imports and material injury to domestic manufacturers.
The post India extends countervailing duty on Malaysian solar glass imports for five years appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Researchers combine agrivoltaics with insect net house
energy-climate.summary:
Researchers in India built two agrivoltaics insect net houses, one with a covered roof and one without, with peppers growing underneath. The team says the arrays generated more energy than standard agrivoltaics.
The post Researchers combine agrivoltaics with insect net house appeared first on pv magazine Global.

school-education.title: What Michigan schools reveal about reversing chronic absenteeism
school-education.summary:
Absenteeism is a huge and seemingly intractable problem for the nation’s public schools. And Michigan has one of the worst attendance rates in the country. That makes it a prime target for researchers. In hundreds of schools, more than 3 out of 5 students were chronically absent before the pandemic. When classes resumed, chronic absenteeism […]

school-education.summary:
Since the start of President Donald Trump’s second term, the White House and Congress have worked aggressively to overhaul federal student financial aid. On July 1, many of those efforts will come to fruition. Student borrowers will begin to see different options for loan repayments and forgiveness, while current students will face new limits on […]

school-education.title: How cutting fruit almost landed a 10-year-old in disciplinary school
school-education.summary:
Juan Lozada’s grandson missed out on almost his entire fifth-grade year at his dual language elementary school in San Antonio, Texas. All because of a child-friendly knife, marketed as safe for ages 3 and up, that the boy brought to school in September 2025 to cut fruit for his lunch.  Lozada was one of the […]

asia-pacific.title: The real story of 11 Skies is not about a failed shopping centre
asia-pacific.summary:
Few things age faster than a business plan built for a world that no longer exists. That may be the most important lesson from the saga of 11 Skies, the vast retail, entertainment and commercial complex beside Hong Kong International Airport that now appears destined for a dramatic reset. Reports that the Airport Authority could take majority control of the project from its private developer have triggered debate over who should bear the cost and who made the wrong bets.
The real story of 11...

asia-pacific.title: South Koreans sweat over US$779,000 air conditioning plan for prisons
asia-pacific.summary:
South Korea’s Ministry of Justice on Tuesday sought to defuse criticism over a 1.2 billion won (US$779,162) plan to install air conditioning in prisons, clarifying the equipment will cool corridors, not inmates’ cells.
The ministry framed the installation as a minimal measure to protect vulnerable inmates and correctional officers from extreme heat, while online critics condemned the taxpayer-funded project as an unfair convenience for criminals.
The ministry said the equipment will be installed

asia-pacific.title: China proposes nuclear-powered floating island to reshape global shipping
asia-pacific.summary:
A Chinese shipbuilder has unveiled a blueprint for a massive, nuclear-powered floating island that will serve as a container transfer terminal and a charging station for vessels.
The floating terminal will be powered by advanced molten salt reactors that use liquefied salt as both a fuel and coolant. These reactors can store vast amounts of thermal energy and cool without the need for water.
Jiangnan Shipyard, a subsidiary of state-owned China State Shipbuilding Corporation, said the complex...

asia-pacific.title: China pancake seller in US plans to run for mayor, generating buzz despite thorny past
asia-pacific.summary:
A Chinese pancake seller in the US has announced plans to run for mayor within three years, despite past controversy over an arrest.
Tang, 31, was born to a rural family in Sichuan, southwestern China, and left school after secondary education. He is the son of a security guard and a cleaner.
After working in sales and the car industry, he moved to the US in 2022, with little English, first taking a job at a used-car showroom.
Two years later, he settled in Los Angeles and began selling savoury.

asia-pacific.title: With fewer public spaces for Hong Kong’s helpers, where can they go to unwind?
asia-pacific.summary:
On a recent stormy Sunday, domestic helpers on their weekly day off were seen hastily erecting tents and laying out plastic sheets under footbridges and inside transport hubs in Hong Kong’s Tsuen Wan district as they sought refuge from the downpour.
Dozens of tents were pitched around the pillars of the footbridges, while other groups crowded onto narrow concrete platforms inside a bus terminus. Many leaned against metal railings or sat on the ground, laying down blankets and carving out spaces.

asia-pacific.title: Fatal crocodile attacks in Indonesia put spotlight on habitat destruction
asia-pacific.summary:
The recent deaths of two men from crocodile attacks in North Sumatra have put a spotlight on Indonesia’s record as the country with the world’s highest number of such killings annually, raising questions over whether habitat destruction is pushing people and wildlife into increasingly dangerous contact.
Environmental groups said the deaths reflected a wider pattern across the archipelago, where logging, plantations and other land-use changes had destabilised ecosystems.
The first incident was on

asia-pacific.title: US citizen and journalist charged with acting as Chinese agent
asia-pacific.summary:
A US citizen who lived and worked for state media in China for several years pleaded guilty on Thursday to acting as a foreign agent for Beijing after allegedly collecting US$100,000 in return for collecting intelligence in the US on “American targets” and American politicians.
According to court documents, Thomas Weir Pauken II worked at the “direction and control” of Chinese officials linked to China’s Ministry of State Security from at least 2019 until February 2026, with someone using the...

asia-pacific.title: Trump says he won’t nominate Pulte as permanent US spy chief after backlash
asia-pacific.summary:
US President ⁠Donald Trump on Thursday indicated ⁠that he would not nominate mortgage regulator ⁠Bill Pulte to be the nation’s intelligence chief once his temporary appointment expires early next year.
His comments follow a backlash against Pulte’s appointment as acting director of national intelligence from Democrats and some key senators of his Republican Party over the ‌Trump loyalist’s lack of national security experience.
Speaking to reporters in the Oval Office, Trump indicated he would...

asia-pacific.title: Trump unveils plan to add ‘Trump promenade’ to Lincoln Memorial
asia-pacific.summary:
President Donald Trump on Thursday announced plans to add a potential “Trump promenade” to the iconic Lincoln Memorial, in his latest grandiose bid to leave his mark on the US capital.
Trump said the walkway would link the huge marble monument, built to commemorate Civil War-era president Abraham Lincoln, to the nearby Potomac River.
“They want to call it the Trump Promenade,” Trump told reporters in the Oval Office as he unveiled the project for the first time. “I don’t know if I want to do...

asia-pacific.title: Chinese satellite company releases images of Nvidia, Apple HQs
asia-pacific.summary:
After it was sanctioned by the US government for allegedly helping Iran, a Chinese satellite company has released high-definition images of the Nvidia and Apple headquarters in California’s Silicon Valley.
They were among photos posted on social media on Monday by Changguang Satellite based in Changchun, in northeast China.
The satellite images – captured by the company’s Jilin-1 constellation – offer a detailed bird’s-eye view of the region.
They include the futuristic Endeavour and Voyager...
